Contributing

Learn how to contribute to the Storyhouse Design System — from component development to documentation.

Overview

Storyhouse is built for teams. Whether you're adding a new component, fixing a bug, or improving documentation, this guide covers the workflow, conventions, and standards you need to know.

Prerequisites

  • Node.js v20+ and npm v10+
  • Familiarity with React 18+, TypeScript (strict mode), and Tailwind CSS
  • Understanding of design token concepts (CSS custom properties, spacing scales)
  • Access to the Figma design file for component specs

Local Development Setup

Terminal 
# Clone the repository
git clone git@github.com:nimphiusBot/storyhouse-design-system.git
cd storyhouse-design-system

# Install all dependencies
npm install

# Build all packages (verify everything works)
npm run build

Working with packages

The monorepo uses npm workspaces. To develop a specific package, you can run its dev server:

Terminal 
# Start Storybook (component development)
cd packages/storybook
npm run storybook

# Start docs site (documentation development)
cd packages/docs
npm run dev

Component Structure

Each component lives in its own directory under packages/components/src/components/{Name}/. Follow this structure when creating new components:

Directory Layout 
packages/components/src/components/{Name}/
├── {Name}.tsx              # Main component implementation
├── {Name}.stories.tsx      # Storybook stories
├── {Name}.test.tsx         # Unit tests (optional)
└── index.ts               # Re-export (optional)

Coding Standards

TypeScript

  • Use strict TypeScript — no any, no @ts-ignore
  • Export a typed props interface named NameProps
  • Use React.forwardRef for all interactive components (wrapping native elements)
  • Spread remaining props onto the underlying DOM element

Styling

  • Use Tailwind CSS for all styling — no CSS modules or styled-components
  • Use cn() from @storyhouse/utils for className merging
  • Support dark mode via dark: prefix on every color/style class
  • Use design tokens (--color-*, --spacing-*) where possible
  • Support className prop on every component for consumer overrides

Component Template

component-name.tsx 
import React from 'react';
import { cn } from '@storyhouse/utils';

export interface CardProps extends React.HTMLAttributes<HTMLDivElement> {
  variant?: 'default' | 'elevated';
  padding?: 'sm' | 'md';
}

const variantStyles = {
  default: 'bg-white border border-gray-200 shadow-sm',
  elevated: 'bg-white border border-gray-100 shadow-md',
};

const paddingStyles = {
  sm: 'p-4',
  md: 'p-6',
};

export const Card = React.forwardRef<HTMLDivElement, CardProps>(
  ({ variant = 'default', padding = 'md', className, children, ...props }, ref) => {
    return (
      <div
        ref={ref}
        className={cn(
          'rounded-xl dark:bg-gray-900',
          variantStyles[variant],
          paddingStyles[padding],
          className
        )}
        {...props}
      >
        {children}
      </div>
    );
  }
);

Card.displayName = 'Card';

Accessibility

  • Use semantic HTML elements (<button>, <input>, etc.)
  • Include aria-* attributes for dynamic states
  • Support keyboard navigation (onKeyDown, tabIndex)
  • Ensure color contrast ratios meet WCAG AA (4.5:1 for text)
  • Test with screen readers (VoiceOver, NVDA)

Storybook Stories

Every component needs Storybook stories for interactive development and documentation. Follow the CSF3 (Component Story Format 3) pattern:

button.stories.tsx 
import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react';
import { Button } from './Button';

const meta = {
  title: 'Components/Button',
  component: Button,
  tags: ['autodocs'],
  argTypes: {
    variant: { control: 'select', options: ['primary', 'secondary', 'ghost', 'danger'] },
    size: { control: 'select', options: ['sm', 'md', 'lg'] }
  },
} satisfies Meta<typeof Button>;

export default meta;
type Story = StoryObj<typeof meta>;

export const Primary: Story = {
  args: { variant: 'primary', children: 'Button' },
};

Testing

Components are tested with Vitest and React Testing Library. Focus on behavior, not implementation:

  • Render tests — verify component renders without crashing
  • Prop tests — each variant, size, and state renders correctly
  • Interaction tests — click handlers, keyboard events
  • Accessibility tests — use @testing-library/jest-dom for accessible queries
  • Aim for 90%+ coverage on all components
Terminal 
# Run tests
npm run test

# Run tests with coverage
npm run test -- --coverage

Pull Request Process

  1. Create a feature branch from main: 
    git checkout -b feat/my-component
  2. Implement your changes following the standards above
  3. Add a changeset for package versioning: 
    npx changeset
# Select packages and describe changes
  4. Write a clear PR description covering:
    • What changed and why
    • Screenshots (for UI changes)
    • Test plan / how to verify
    • Breaking changes (if any)
  5. Request review from at least one maintainer
  6. Address review feedback and squash merge when approved

Documentation

When adding or updating components, always update the docs site:

  • Create a new page at packages/docs/src/pages/components/name.astro
  • Add the page to the sidebar in DocLayout.astro
  • Add a component card to packages/docs/src/pages/components.astro
  • Add a search entry in SearchModal.astro
  • Add documentation for any new props in a PropsTable

Versioning

Storyhouse follows Semantic Versioning (semver):

  • Major — breaking changes, redesigns, removed APIs
  • Minor — new components, new features, token additions
  • Patch — bug fixes, documentation, minor style tweaks
ESC
Type to search components, guides, tokens, and more…

Storyhouse Design System

Built for scale, precision, and exceptional developer experience.
